mode_selection_guide.overview: Each pipeline stage that calls askBob should pin a specific custom mode. The mode encapsulates *how* Bob should respond — output format, what to prioritize, what permissions Bob has. Selecting the right mode is the difference between getting a structured risk-ranked review and getting a wall of generic prose.

available_modes.description: Two categories live in .bob/custom_modes.yaml: - solution-* modes: pre-built reference modes added by lab contributors. - pipeline-* modes (and any other slug): participant-authored modes added during the workshop (Lab 1 has participants build pipeline-git-diff-overview).

mode.purpose: Analyzes git diffs to provide concise, actionable summaries of code changes in CI/CD pipelines. Categorizes changes by type (features, bug fixes, refactoring, configuration, tests, documentation), identifies potential risks, and highlights breaking changes. Output is formatted for Jenkins console logs - plain text, no markdown tables.
mode.inputs: Git diff content (as text or file path)
mode.example_call:
def diffContent = sh(script: 'git diff origin/main...HEAD', returnStdout: true).trim() def prompt = """ Analyze the following git diff and provide: - Executive summary of changes - Risk assessment (high/medium/low) - Key areas requiring review - Breaking changes or API modifications
Diff: ${diffContent} """ def analysis = askBob(prompt, 'pipeline-git-diff-overview')

mode.purpose: Diagnoses failing JUnit/Surefire reports. Identifies root cause, suggests a fix, and groups related failures.
mode.inputs: Surefire/Failsafe report contents (concatenated text reports, or raw stack traces)

mode.purpose: General Java code review with line-level recommendations. More detailed than pipeline-git-diff-overview — slower, deeper.
mode.inputs: git diff or full source files

rule.if: The stage analyzes a git diff for a PR-style risk overview
The stage analyzes failing test output (Surefire / Failsafe / JUnit)
The stage wants a thorough, line-level Java code review
None of the above match — you're doing something this catalog doesn't cover
rule.then: Build a new mode with Mode Writer rather than reusing one that *almost* fits. Each pipeline stage benefits from a mode tuned to its inputs and output expectations. See the labs for the prompt-then-promote pattern.

convention.name: pipeline-_prefix_for_pipeline_modes
convention.description: Modes invoked from the pipeline (read-only, narrow permissions, plain-text output suited to a Jenkins console) are prefixed `pipeline-`. This signals they're CI-side, not IDE-side. IDE-side modes (broader permissions, used in the Bob IDE during a task) don't need a prefix or use a `solution-` prefix if pre-built.

convention.name: read-only_for_pipeline_modes
convention.description: A mode invoked from the pipeline should declare `groups: [read]` only. Pipelines run unattended; minimum permissions reduce blast radius if a mode is misconfigured.

convention.name: plain_text_output_for_pipeline_modes
convention.description: Jenkins console doesn't render markdown tables or ANSI colors well. Pipeline modes should produce plain text with simple section headers (Summary, Risk, Watch for). Save fancy formatting for IDE modes.

combinations.description: Most pipelines run one Bob analysis per stage. If you want multiple lenses (overview + deep review + security), use separate stages — they archive separate artifacts and the build log stays scannable.

anti_pattern.name: reusing_an_almost_right_mode
anti_pattern.description: Picking a mode that *kind of* fits because building a new one feels like work.
anti_pattern.impact: The mode's output format and priorities don't match what the stage needs. You end up writing long prompts to override the mode — at which point the mode is doing nothing useful.
anti_pattern.fix: Build a new mode with Mode Writer; it takes 5 minutes.

anti_pattern.name: default_mode_in_production
anti_pattern.description: Calling askBob without a mode (relying on Bob's default) for a stage that runs on every push.
anti_pattern.impact: Output drifts shape from run to run. Different commits get different formats. Hard to consume downstream.
anti_pattern.fix: Pin a custom mode for any stage that runs on every push. Default mode is fine for one-off exploration in the IDE; not for repeatable CI.

anti_pattern.name: ide_mode_in_pipeline
anti_pattern.description: Using an IDE-side mode (broad permissions, markdown-heavy output) in the pipeline.
anti_pattern.impact: Wider permissions than needed; output is harder to read in a Jenkins console.
anti_pattern.fix: Build a pipeline-* twin if you need similar behavior in CI — read-only, plain-text output.

mapping.mode: Build a new pipeline-* mode with Mode Writer
